1: The opening verse reverses the darkness of chapter 8's closing: no more gloom where gloom once reigned. The MKT preserves the Hebrew contrast structure without softening it. The geographical references — Zebulun, Naphtali, the way of the sea — anchor the promise in the northern territories that fell first to Assyrian conquest, making the fulfillment in Matt 4:13-16 geographically precise.
2: The people who walked in darkness have seen a great light. The Hebrew haalekh (walking) is a participle indicating habitual action — they were characteristically darkness-walkers; the light-seeing is a prophetic perfect (seen as already accomplished). The MKT's past tense have seen captures this prophetic confidence. Matthew 4:16 cites this verse for Jesus' Galilean ministry.
3: The joy-multiplication imagery draws on harvest and battle-spoil celebrations — the two moments in Israelite life when communal joy was most intense. The textual note: some manuscripts read not multiplied the joy (lo = no), while the Qere reads to him (lo = to him). The MKT follows the Qere, reading an increase of joy, which fits the surrounding context of abundance.
4: The breaking of the yoke, staff, and rod evokes the Midianite defeat (Judg 7) where Gideon's 300 routed an overwhelming army. The MKT's the day of Midian is an allusion requiring the reader to know the Gideon narrative. The instrument of oppression — the rod — will become the instrument of the Messiah's rule in 9:6, but transformed from oppression into governance.
5: Every warrior's boot and blood-soaked garment will be burned as fuel — an image of warfare's complete abolition. The MKT renders sogar as soaked rather than rolled in — the former emphasizes the saturation, giving visceral weight to the contrast with what follows. The burning of war equipment appears again in Ezek 39:9-10 for the eschatological end of warfare.
6: Four throne-names in apposition: the MKT renders Pele-Yoetz as Wonderful Counselor (two words, not one name), following the majority reading. The name Mighty God (El Gibbor) uses El — the word for deity — applied to the royal child, a significant theological statement. Everlasting Father (Abi-Ad) means father of eternity/perpetuity — a protector-king role. Prince of Peace (Sar-Shalom) uses shalom in its full sense of well-being and wholeness, not merely absence of conflict.
7: Three key terms cluster here: mishpat (justice/right order), tsedaqah (righteousness with social-justice force), and shalom (peace). The MKT renders tsedaqah as righteousness — a defensible choice, though the prophetic context (justice for the poor, governance of the oppressed) suggests the social-legal dimension is primary. Olam is rendered everlasting — absolute timelessness rather than age-long duration — following the NT application to Christ's eternal kingdom (Luke 1:33). The YHWH-name note applies: the zeal of the LORD of hosts identifies the divine personal name as the guarantor of this promise.
8: The word (dabar) sent against Jacob — word as active agent of divine judgment, not mere communication. The MKT captures this with sent a word rather than spoken against: the word goes out and accomplishes its purpose (cf. Isa 55:11). The word-as-weapon motif will recur in 11:4 (the breath of his lips) and Rev 19:15.
9: Yada (know/acknowledge) is flagged as dispute-level 2. The verse says the people know — but in context it is a proud, self-sufficient knowing: we know what to do to rebuild. The MKT's will know it preserves ambiguity between simple awareness and the arrogant self-confidence Isaiah is critiquing.
10: The bricks-to-dressed-stone / sycamores-to-cedars contrast illustrates the arrogance of upgrading rather than repenting after judgment. The sycamore was a common cheap timber; the cedar was the premium timber of temples and palaces. The people respond to God's corrective discipline with a building program, not a return to the LORD — a pattern Paul warns against in treating divine patience as license (Rom 2:4).
11: The divine name YHWH is flagged here. The LORD raises up the adversaries of Rezin — the personal name of God as the one who orchestrates the nations is the MKT's way of maintaining the intimate, covenantal dimension of judgment. It is not an abstract deity but the covenant God who raises up enemies to discipline his people.
12: Devoured with open mouth — the image of Israel as prey surrounded on all sides (Syrians in front, Philistines behind). The refrain his hand is stretched out still (repeated in 9:17, 9:21, 10:4) creates a structural urgency: the divine hand of judgment does not withdraw because the people do not return. The hand stretched out in judgment will be stretched out in healing and deliverance in 11:11.
13: They have not returned to him who struck them, nor have they sought the LORD of hosts. The failure of judgment to produce repentance is a recurring diagnostic in Isaiah (cf. 1:5). The divine name YHWH-Sabaoth (LORD of hosts) is flagged — the military title for the God who commands the armies of heaven, a title implying that Israel's enemies are ultimately fighting against the divine Commander of all armies.
14: Head and tail, palm branch and reed — the Hebrew idiom for high and low, top and bottom of society. All ranks will be cut off on a single day (yom echad). The day of comprehensive judgment is a precursor to the Day of the LORD theme developed in chapter 13. The divine name YHWH is flagged — this cutting-off is an act of the personal covenant God, not fate.
15: The identification of the honored elder as the head and the lying prophet as the tail is a key hermeneutical moment: Isaiah is interpreting the metaphor. The false prophet who teaches lies is the most culpable part of the leadership structure — those who speak in God's name but do not speak God's truth bear the greatest responsibility for the people's misdirection (cf. Jer 23; Ezek 34).
16: Those whom they lead are swallowed up. The Hebrew mevulshalim means entangled, confused — the MKT's swallowed up is a stronger rendering that captures the total engulfment the false guidance produces. The young men and orphans and widows — the vulnerable who most need honest leadership — are specifically named as those the LORD will not have compassion on, a shocking reversal of divine mercy that underlines the severity of the crisis.
17: The divine withholding of compassion on the young men and orphans is one of the most jarring statements in Isaiah. The MKT preserves the shock: has no compassion — not a softening like cannot rejoice in. In the context of 9:13 (they have not returned), this is the logical consequence: persistent refusal of repentance eventually exhausts the patience of the compassionate God. The refrain appears again.
18: Wickedness burning like fire — an image that moves from individual wickedness to consuming societal conflagration. The briers and thorns of unproductive land catch first, then the dense forest, then the smoke rises in columns. The image of fire-as-judgment consuming first the worthless vegetation and then the great forest is developed in 10:17-19, where the Assyrian empire is the forest destroyed by the LORD's fire.
19: Through the wrath of the LORD of hosts the land is scorched, and the people are like fuel for the fire. The divine name YHWH-Sabaoth is flagged again. Fuel for the fire — the Hebrew esh-oklah-esh is a striking pun: fire is being consumed by fire. Society in breakdown devours itself. The MKT's like fuel for the fire preserves the consuming image without losing the self-devouring element.
20: Snatch food on the right, eat on the left, still hungry — the insatiability of self-consuming sin. The tribal conflict that follows (Manasseh and Ephraim against each other and against Judah) is the domestic expression of the cosmic spiral of mutual destruction. When society forgets the LORD, it turns its violence inward. The refrain of the outstretched hand closes the chapter and opens it to 10:1.
21: The closing refrain — his hand is stretched out still — is the third occurrence, creating a drumbeat of unrelenting divine attention. The hand that is stretched out in judgment is the same hand that will be stretched out for the second time in 11:11 to reclaim the remnant. The outstretched hand is the posture of both judgment and salvation; which you encounter depends on whether you turn toward or away from the LORD.
